content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR
POLITICAL
PRACTICES COMMISSION
April 24,
1996
Ann Cochran Heywood
Deputy Secretary
Technology Development
California Environmental Protection Agency
555 Capitol Mall,
Suite
525
Sacramento,
California
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—96-136
Dear Ms.
Heywood:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) 1
QUESTION
May you remain an employee of Thermatrix on leave of absence
while serving as
a public official with Cal/EPA?
CONCLUSION
Your specific question does not fall within the jurisdiction
of the Political Reform Act.
Your economic interest in
Thermatrix,
however,
may implicate the Act’s conflict—of—interest
provisions as discussed below.
FACTS
You are a former employee of Thermatrix currently working at
the California Environmental Protection Agency.
You currently own
stock in Thermatrix.
As a result of your ownership of Thermatrix
stock,
when you began your employment at Cal/EPA you declared your
conflict-of—interest with Thermatrix and formally disqualified
yourself from participating in any matter involving Thermatrix.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
Commission regulations
appear at Title 2,
Sections
18000—18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—136
Page
2
Thermatrix is currently planning an Initial Public Offering
(“IPO”).
Recently,
you have discovered that as a result of a
complex issue involving your stock options in Thermatrix,
you will
be subject to significant financial penalty if you return to
Thermatrix after the IPO.
You do,
however, wish to continue your
period of public service at Cal/EPA.
Accordingly,
you hope to
remain a Thermatrix employee on leave of absence while you work at
Cal/EPA.
ANALYSIS
As we discussed during our telephone conversation on
April 19,
1996,
issues of incompatible activities are not within
the jurisdiction of the Political Reform Act.
Therefore,
I
suggested that you contact counsel at Cal/EPA and the Attorney
General’s office regarding the doctrine of incompatible
activities.
As a designated employee of Cal/EPA,
you are considered a
“public official” under the Act,
and are therefore subject to the
disqualification provisions of the Act.
As such,
Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in,
or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he or she knows or has reason to know he or she
has a financial interest.
An official has a financia1 interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status,
aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.


File No.
A—96—136
Page
3
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or agent for
a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
Section 87103.
In addition,
Commission regulations provide that an official
must disqualify himself or herself from any decision which “will
result in the personal expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities of
the official or his or her immediate family increasing or
decreasing by at least $250 in any 12-month period.”
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (4).)
Accordingly,
you may not make, participate in making,
or
attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on any of your economic interests.
This applies
to investments or income obtained within the past
12 months as
well as
income currently being received by the official.
You have already disclosed your investment interest in
Thermatrix and disqualified yourself from decisions involving the
company.
If you determine that it is not an incompatible activity
for you to retain your position at Cal/EPA while you are on a
leave of absence from Thermatrix,
you must continue to disqualify
yourself from any decision that will have
a material financial
effect on Thermatrix.
If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak

QUESTION: May you remain an employee of Thermatrix on leave of absence
while serving as
a public official with Cal/EPA?

CONCLUSION: Your specific question does not fall within the jurisdiction
of the Political Reform Act.
Your economic interest in
Thermatrix,
however,

provisions as discussed below.
